content.problemStatement:
In this task, you will create a function that attempts to open a file, counts the number of attempts made, and ensures that the count is always updated and returned, regardless of whether the file was successfully opened or an exception occurred.

The function should take a filename as input and try to open it. If successful, it should read the first line of the file. If an exception occurs (e.g., file not found), it should catch the exception. In both cases, it should increment a counter for the number of attempts made.

Use a 'finally' clause to ensure that the counter is always incremented and returned, regardless of whether an exception occurred or not.

For example, if the input is "existing_file.txt" and the file exists, the function should return 1 (indicating one attempt was made). If the input is "non_existent_file.txt" and the file doesn't exist, the function should also return 1.

Your task is to complete the 'open_file_counter' function to implement this behavior.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10) – the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single filename (a non‑empty string without spaces).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line – the number of attempts made to open the file (always 1).

sampleTestCases.explanation: Two test cases are processed. For each filename the function increments the counter exactly once, so both outputs are 1.
A single filename is given; regardless of whether README.md exists, the counter is incremented once.
Three independent attempts are made; each finally block adds one to the counter, yielding 1 for every test case.

solution.approach: For every filename we perform exactly one attempt to open the file. The attempt is wrapped in a try‑catch (or equivalent) block so that any error (e.g., file not found) is caught and ignored. A counter variable is initialised to 0 before the attempt. In a finally clause we increment the counter and return it. Because the finally block is executed whether the open succeeds or an exception is thrown, the returned value is always 1. The algorithm is O(T) time and O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – one constant‑time operation per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few scalar variables are used.
